rows.title: HmbBfDI Issues €775,000 in GDPR Fines for First Nine Months of 2025, Including €492,000 Against Financial Firm Over Automated Decision Failures
rows.summary:
The Hamburgische Beauftragte für Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit (HmbBfDI) has imposed a total of approximately €775,000 in fines under the General Data Protection Regulation across 15 concluded proceedings in the first nine months of 2025, with the largest penalty of €492,000 levied against an unnamed financial sector company for failures relating to automated individual decision-making (Art. 22 GDPR).

On 30 September 2025, the HmbBfDI published an interim review disclosing that the financial sector company had rejected credit card applications from several customers with sound credit ratings using fully automated, algorithm-based processes without human intervention. When affected customers requested explanations for the rejections, the HmbBfDI finds that the company failed to adequately fulfil its statutory information and access obligations, including the requirement to provide meaningful information about the logic involved in automated decision-making (Art. 13, 14 and 15 GDPR). The HmbBfDI notes that such automated procedures are permissible only under strict conditions and carry heightened obligations for controllers. The HmbBfDI states that the company's extensive cooperation throughout both the administrative and penalty proceedings, and the substantial improvements it made to its processes for handling data subject rights in automated decision-making, were given considerable weight as mitigating factors in the calculation of the fine. The company accepted the penalty notice.

Beyond the financial sector case, the HmbBfDI finds that enforcement activity focused on two further areas. The HmbBfDI determined that three companies were fined in the lower five-figure range each for sending promotional emails to customers without valid consent (Art. 6(1) GDPR). A retail company received a fine of €195,000 after it repeatedly and over a prolonged period failed to respond to data subject rights requests within the required time limits following a postal advertising campaign conducted through third-party service providers (Art. 12 GDPR). The HmbBfDI imposed six fines on employees of the Hamburg police and other public authorities who conducted unauthorised queries of personal data held in official databases, and one hospital employee was fined for accessing a colleague's patient records without any involvement in that person's treatment. Thomas Fuchs, the HmbBfDI, states that where companies systematically fail to respond adequately to information and access requests, a perceptible sanction is required, and that this applies in particular to opaque structures such as address trading or complex decision algorithms, and increasingly to the use of artificial intelligence.
rows.body_name: Hamburgische Beauftragte fur Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit

rows.title: HmbBfDI calls on German federal government to designate AI market surveillance authorities after EU AI Act deadline passes
rows.summary:
The Hamburgische Beauftragte für Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit (HmbBfDI) has called on the German federal government to designate competent market surveillance authorities under the EU AI Act, following Germany's failure to meet the 2 August 2025 designation deadline imposed on EU member states.

On 31 July 2025, the HmbBfDI, Thomas Fuchs, issued a public appeal to the federal government to act without delay in designating the AI market surveillance authorities required under the EU AI Regulation (AI Act). The HmbBfDI notes that Germany has not enacted a national AI Act implementing law, leaving the designation process incomplete as the statutory deadline expired.

The HmbBfDI finds that EU member states were required to designate competent market surveillance authorities by 2 August 2025 under the AI Act. The authority notes that Germany's failure to meet this deadline means that businesses and public bodies currently lack a binding point of contact for questions relating to AI Act compliance. Thomas Fuchs states that this situation represents a disadvantage for Germany as a location for AI innovation. The HmbBfDI further notes that the AI Act imposes demanding requirements on designated authorities, including independence and adequate technical, financial and human resources, specifically staff with expertise in artificial intelligence, data law and product safety law.

The HmbBfDI observes that the AI Act already defines the role of data protection supervisory authorities in certain sectors. Under Article 74(8) of the AI Act, the HmbBfDI notes that data protection supervisory authorities are designated as the competent market surveillance authorities for high-risk AI systems deployed in areas particularly sensitive to fundamental rights, including law enforcement, migration and the administration of justice. The HmbBfDI notes that responsibility for AI systems used in other areas of the public sector, such as education, remains unresolved pending national implementing legislation. Despite the absence of a national legal framework, the HmbBfDI states that it is proactively building internal competence and training staff for the assessment of AI systems in preparation for the moment of formal statutory designation. The authority additionally notes that new transparency obligations for AI model providers under the AI Act apply from 2 August 2025.
rows.body_name: Hamburgische Beauftragte fur Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit

rows.title: HmbBfDI assumes Data Act supervisory role for personal data from connected devices from 12 September 2025
rows.summary:
The Hamburgische Beauftragte für Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit (HmbBfDI) has confirmed its supervisory competence under the EU Data Act for matters involving personal data, with the regulation entering into application on 12 September 2025, on the basis of Art. 37(3) Data Act.

On 9 September 2025, the HmbBfDI published a press release setting out the new rights and oversight responsibilities arising from the Data Act's application date of 12 September 2025. The authority notes that the Data Act creates access rights to sensor data generated by connected devices, ranging from electric toothbrushes to wind turbines, for both users of those devices and eligible third parties, provided the conditions set out in the Data Act are met, data protection law does not preclude access, and trade secrets are preserved.

The HmbBfDI states that, under Art. 37(3) Data Act, data protection authorities are designated as the competent supervisory body for Data Act compliance where personal data are involved. The authority finds that its remit covers, in particular: access to personal data held by manufacturers; switching between cloud and data processing service providers; the protection of confidentiality through technical and organisational measures at the receiving party; and transparency obligations. The HmbBfDI notes that it is able to enforce these rights by means of orders and that infringements may, in part, be subject to fines. Alternatively, the authority states, rights may be pursued independently through civil courts. The HmbBfDI has published detailed guidance on the rights and obligations arising from the Data Act, its interaction with data protection law, and supervisory enforcement.

The authority confirms that any natural or legal person may lodge a complaint with the HmbBfDI where there are grounds to believe that a Hamburg-based undertaking has infringed their Data Act rights in relation to personal data. The HmbBfDI notes that each complaint is handled by the departmental unit responsible for data protection supervision of the relevant controller, in line with the objective in Art. 37(3) Data Act of ensuring consistent assessment of data use under both the GDPR and the Data Act. The authority further notes that its Data Act supervisory competence is confined to cases involving personal data, and that, for non-personal data, the German federal legislature has yet to designate a competent authority, meaning such claims remain limited to the civil courts for the time being.
rows.body_name: Hamburgische Beauftragte fur Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit

rows.title: Court of Justice of the EU Rules Hosting Providers Bear GDPR Responsibility for User-Published Content in Russmedia Case
rows.summary:
The Court of Justice of the European Union ruled on 2 December 2025 that a hosting provider can bear joint controllership under the GDPR for unlawful content published by users on its platform.

On 2 December 2025, the Court of Justice of the European Union delivered its judgment in Case C-492/23 Russmedia. The case concerned an advertisement placed on an online marketplace without consent, using a woman's photograph and telephone number to offer alleged sexual services. The Court finds that the hosting provider bore data protection responsibility for user-published content on the platform even without direct influence over the advertisement's content.

The Court determined that decisions taken in connection with operating the platform, specifically concerning the purposes and means of processing personal data, were sufficient to engage GDPR obligations under Article 4(7) GDPR. The Court notes that the hosting provider's terms of service reserved rights to use, distribute, transmit, reproduce, modify, translate, pass to partners and delete published content at any time without providing reasons. The Court finds that a hosting provider, acting as controller, is required to identify content containing special category data under Article 9(1) GDPR before publication, verify whether the person whose data appears in an advertisement is the publishing user, and confirm that a valid legal basis or consent exists where that is not the case.

The Court notes that the liability privilege under Article 6 DSA, which applies once a hosting provider gains actual knowledge of unlawful content via the notice-and-action mechanism, does not limit or reduce data subjects' rights under the GDPR. The Court determines that GDPR responsibility arises prior to publication and does not depend on notification of a violation.
rows.body_name: Hamburgische Beauftragte fur Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit

rows.title: HmbBfDI Sets Out GPAI Provider Obligations Under EU AI Act from 2 August 2025
rows.summary:
The Hamburgische Beauftragte für Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit (HmbBfDI) has published guidance confirming that new transparency and compliance obligations for providers of general-purpose AI (GPAI) models under the EU AI Act (Regulation (EU) 2024/1689) entered into force on 2 August 2025.

On 31 July 2025, the HmbBfDI issued a statement setting out the scope and timeline of obligations now binding on GPAI model providers under the AI Act. The authority notes that these rules build upon the prohibitions on certain AI practices applicable since February 2025 and the AI literacy requirement already in force.

The HmbBfDI sets out that, for GPAI models placed on the European market after 2 August 2025, providers are required to create technical documentation, declare compliance with EU copyright law, publish a detailed summary of training data used, and provide information on model functionality. For high-capability models presenting systemic risk, the authority notes that providers are additionally required to test for vulnerabilities and risks and to report serious security incidents. The HmbBfDI observes that the European Commission has published guidelines to assist providers in implementing these requirements.

The HmbBfDI finds that the new obligations produce a corresponding empowerment for companies and public authorities that integrate GPAI models into their own AI systems, allowing those deployers to demand from model providers the information necessary for their own compliance. The HmbBfDI states that this enables deployers to better assess the GPAI models they use, minimise risks, and fulfil data protection due diligence obligations. On the implementation timeline, the authority confirms that providers of models already available before 2 August 2025 benefit from a transition period running until 2 August 2027. For enforcement, the HmbBfDI notes that the AI Office, situated within the Commission, is able to launch investigations and demand corrective measures from August 2025, while the Commission's power to impose fines and other sanctions under the AI Act applies only from 2 August 2026.
rows.body_name: Hamburgische Beauftragte fur Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit

rows.title: Hamburg DPA Fines Debt Collection Firm €900,000 for Retaining Personal Data Up to Five Years Beyond Deletion Deadlines
rows.summary:
The Hamburgische Beauftragte für Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit (HmbBfDI) has imposed a fine of €900,000 (EUR 900,000) on a Hamburg-based debt collection service provider for retaining personal data without a legal basis for up to five years beyond applicable deletion deadlines, in breach of Art. 5(1)(a) and Art. 6(1) GDPR.

On 12 November 2024, the HmbBfDI announced the outcome of a sector-wide audit of companies with a strong market presence in the debt collection industry. The HmbBfDI notes that Hamburg is a leading European location for this sector and that data processed about defaulting debtors tends to be particularly sensitive, as it is regularly shared with third parties including credit reference agencies and address investigation services.

The HmbBfDI conducted the audit independently of individual complaints, examining how debtors' data was stored and processed across the sector. The authority sent detailed questionnaires to the companies examined and requested documents including records of processing activities, lists of security measures, and sample correspondence. The HmbBfDI subsequently conducted on-site inspections at a number of the companies. The HmbBfDI finds that for the majority of those audited, there was a high degree of professionalism and sensitivity, and the authority notes that improvements in transparency towards data subjects were achieved through dialogue, with particular focus on the formulation of meaningful data access responses under Art. 15 GDPR and on timely access procedures.

In one case, the HmbBfDI determines that the company stored a six-figure number of personal data records without any legal basis until at least mid-November 2023, with some records remaining in the company's database up to five years after the expiry of the statutory retention period, in violation of Art. 5(1)(a) and Art. 6(1) GDPR. The HmbBfDI finds that although the data were not passed to third parties during this period, their continued retention was unlawful. The HmbBfDI states that the fine of €900,000 is final and legally binding. The HmbBfDI notes that the company admitted the violation, accepted the fine, and cooperated professionally with the authority during the remediation process, which the authority states was taken into account when setting the penalty amount. The HmbBfDI further notes that a second audited company was found to have comparable deficiencies relating to deletion obligations, and that proceedings against that company remain ongoing.
rows.body_name: Hamburgische Beauftragte fur Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit

rows.title: HmbBfDI Annual Report 2023: Complaint Cases Rise 20%, Data Breach Notifications Hit 925
rows.summary:
The Hamburgische Beauftragte für Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit (HmbBfDI) published its 32nd annual data protection report for 2023, recording a 20 per cent increase in complaint cases, 925 data breach notifications, and findings that deletion failures occur across multiple sectors.

On 15 April 2024, HmbBfDI Commissioner Thomas Fuchs presented the 2023 annual data protection report to Hamburg Parliament President Carola Veit. The HmbBfDI reports that complaint proceedings increased to 2,537 cases in 2023, compared with 2,160 in the prior year, representing a rise of approximately 20 per cent. The authority attributes this growth largely to complaints relating to products and services offered by Meta and Google, reflecting HmbBfDI's lead supervisory role in Germany for those companies. The HmbBfDI reports that data breach notifications also increased, with 925 reports recorded in 2023 compared with 859 the previous year. Of those, the authority notes that 235 concerned hacker attacks, up from 227 in 2022. The HmbBfDI initiated 20 fine proceedings during the reporting year.

The HmbBfDI finds that failures by organisations to implement adequate deletion practices constitute a recurring, cross-sectoral problem. The authority observes that personal data, including copies of identity documents and financial records, is frequently retained after the purpose for which it was collected has lapsed, in breach of the storage limitation principle (Art. 5(1)(e) GDPR) and the requirement to erase data without undue delay (Art. 17 GDPR). The authority identifies this pattern across sectors ranging from debt collection services to the hospitality industry. The report also addresses AI supervision: Commissioner Fuchs states that data protection supervisory authorities are the appropriate bodies to oversee compliance with the EU AI Regulation, given their existing familiarity with IT systems processing personal data.
rows.body_name: Hamburgische Beauftragte fur Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit

rows.title: HmbBfDI Confirms Court Ruling on Guest Checkout Exception for Online Marketplaces
rows.summary:
The Hamburgische Beauftragte für Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit (HmbBfDI) has confirmed that the Hanseatisches Oberlandesgericht Hamburg upheld a ruling that online marketplace operators such as Otto.de are not required to offer guest checkout where compensatory data minimisation measures are in place (Art. 5(1)(c) GDPR).

On 12 March 2025, the HmbBfDI published a press release reporting that the Hanseatisches Oberlandesgericht Hamburg ruled on 27 February 2025 (Az. 5 U 30/24) in favour of Otto.de, dismissing an action by Verbraucherzentrale Nordrhein-Westfalen seeking to compel guest ordering. The court confirmed a first-instance judgment of the Landgericht Hamburg.

The HmbBfDI finds that a general rule established by the Konferenz der unabhängigen Datenschutzaufsichtsbehörden des Bundes und der Länder (DSK) on 24 March 2022 requires online retailers to offer guest ordering to comply with the data minimisation principle. The authority notes that compelling customers to create permanent accounts is typically incompatible with data protection law, as such accounts contain unnecessary personal data and expose information to security risks.

The HmbBfDI determines that Otto.de satisfied conditions for an exception to the guest checkout obligation as an online marketplace hosting third-party sellers. The authority finds that Otto.de demonstrated that customer service, including returns organisation, operates more efficiently through a unified customer account. The HmbBfDI notes that Otto.de adopted alternative data minimisation measures, including automated account deletion following inactivity and data removal after warranty expiry. The authority states that whilst the DSK resolution is not binding on courts, both judicial decisions treated it as soft law. The HmbBfDI finds that other platforms claiming marketplace status must substantiate that claim with concrete usage data, as general assertions do not establish entitlement to the exception.
rows.body_name: Hamburgische Beauftragte fur Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit

rows.title: Hamburg DPA Issues Guidance on Data Act Obligations and Intersection with GDPR Ahead of September 2025 Application Date
rows.summary:
The Hamburgische Beauftragte für Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit (HmbBfDI) has published guidance on the obligations imposed by Regulation (EU) 2023/2854 on harmonised rules on fair access to and use of data (the Data Act) and its interaction with the General Data Protection Regulation, ahead of the Act's application date of September 2025.

On 29 April 2025, the Hamburgische Beauftragte für Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit published an information paper setting out the core requirements of the Data Act and the implications for data protection practice within organisations. No fines are reported in this publication.

The HmbBfDI explains that the Data Act aims to break up existing monopolies over data generated by connected devices, covering the Internet of Things, including connected vehicles, household appliances, televisions, and medical and fitness devices. The authority notes that, under the current landscape, manufacturers routinely receive usage and wear data from such devices, while users and third parties such as independent repair workshops and spare parts producers have typically lacked access to that data. The Data Act, the HmbBfDI explains, is intended to enable both individual consumers and business customers who have purchased internet-connected products to determine for themselves to whom the data generated during use is disclosed, including through licence agreements with third parties.

On the relationship between the Data Act and the GDPR, the HmbBfDI finds that personal data may only be shared with third parties where data protection law does not preclude it (Art. 3(1) Data Act). The authority states that in many cases there will be no legal basis for the disclosure of personal business data, meaning that the core obligations of the Data Act will in practice predominantly concern non-personal data. The HmbBfDI determines that the assessment of whether a personal data element is present, and whether any disclosure would be compatible with the GDPR, can only meaningfully and bindingly be decided by the personnel within an organisation responsible for data protection. The authority further notes that its own role is expanding: where disputes arise between undertakings over access to customer data, the data protection authority may be required to direct that access be granted in accordance with the conditions of the Data Act. Where access is technically feasible and relevant, the HmbBfDI notes it is to be provided directly via an interface or customer portal (Art. 3(1) Data Act), with individual access requests required where that is not the case.
rows.body_name: Hamburgische Beauftragte fur Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit
